fuzzy_prd:
We need the full Kubernetes discovery/observation behavior covered by the tests: refreshing namespaces, applying namespace watch events, refreshing served resources with selectors and capability filters, and applying resource-definition change events. Resource-definition events are one feature, not the entire task. The adapter should route by action and emit deterministic namespace/resource readiness, notification, ambiguity, warnings, and API-call traces.

One other thing the team asked about: when an event comes in with the type field null (absent), that’s just the initial list phase and we should fully ignore it. That means no waiter notification, no served-resource update, and no API discovery calls at all. So the output in that case is exactly notified=no, resources unchanged (empty if initial_resources is empty), core_namespaces_ready=no, api_calls= . That same ignore-vs-apply pattern is the one we want in both apply_namespace_event and apply_resource_definition_event: null-type events are ignored with notified=no and no state change, and non-null-type events do the normal state update and notify with notified=yes.

Also, the output needs to spell out discovery traffic with api_calls=<comma-separated list of API path strings> in the exact order the calls happened during the rescan. For an extension group rescan, that includes /apis and /apis/<group>/<version> for each version of the affected group. For a core API rescan, it’s /api and /api/v1. If nothing was fetched, like on an initial-list event, then it stays empty as api_calls=.

For the group-removal case, if we rescan /apis and the affected group is no longer in the groups list, we should treat that group as now having zero resources and remove any previously served resources from that group out of the catalog. Resources from other groups stay as they were.

On the core side, the backbone resources for group='' use their own discovery path, /api and /api/v1, and the output should always carry core_namespaces_ready=yes|no. That flag is no for an initial-list event, when no core API rescan happened, or when the core API response does not include namespaces. It only flips to yes after a later event triggers a core API rescan and that resource list includes namespaces.

Last piece was the capability filter behavior in feature 3.3: to be watchable, a resource needs list+watch verbs. If handler_mode is background_task or timer, it also needs patch. If those checks fail, we should emit non_watchable_resource or non_patchable_resource warnings respectively.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with action) -> stdout namespace/resource discovery state and notification lines

trigger_keywords: notify waiters on follow-up event
notified=yes condition
when does notified flag become yes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The notified=yes output is produced when the event type is non-null (i.e., ADDED, MODIFIED, or DELETED) AND the event affects a namespace that matches the configured patterns OR when any follow-up resource-definition event causes a rescan. Concretely for apply_resource_definition_event: any non-null event type produces notified=yes regardless of whether the rescan finds matching resources.

trigger_keywords: selector matching after rescan
served resources after group refresh
which resources end up in output after event
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After rescanning, the library applies the same selector matching, capability filtering (list/watch required; patch required for background_task/timer handler modes), and ambiguity resolution rules as in refresh_resources. The resources= output line lists group/version/plural entries for all resources that pass these filters, in the same format as feature 3. If no resources match, resources= is empty.

trigger_keywords: group scope of rescan
which group is rescanned from event
spec.group field in resource definition event
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The API group to rescan is taken from the event's spec.group field. The library fetches /apis to get the group's versions, then fetches /apis/<group>/<version> for each version listed. Only resources belonging to the affected group are replaced in the served catalog; resources from other groups are preserved, matching the partial-group-replace behavior from feature 3 (the group-scoped refresh).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the core backbone ones that needs specific handling separate from the extension group flow
context_pointers.actual_meaning: When the event's spec.group corresponds to the core API (group=''), discovery uses /api and /api/v1 instead of /apis and /apis/<group>/<version>, and the core_namespaces_ready flag is set to yes if the /api/v1 resources list contains an entry with name='namespaces' — see feature4_resource_definition_event_processing.json cases for core API

context_pointers.vague_reference: same pattern as before — signaling to waiters
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The notified field in the output mirrors the namespace watch event notified=yes|no contract from Feature 2; for resource definition events, notified=yes for any non-null event type, notified=no for null-type initial-list events

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details about that aspect in the current requirements. Could you check the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature4_resource_definition_event_processing.json and the patterns established in features 2 and 3 for the closest analogous behavior? If you have a more specific question about the output format, event filtering logic, API call tracing, or the core vs extension group handling, feel free to ask and I can clarify.

feature1_1_namespace_refresh_from_lists..description: Refresh namespace membership from listed namespace objects, adding matching names, preserving prior matches across incremental refreshes, removing deletion-marked names, and ignoring names outside configured patterns.

feature1_2_namespace_refresh_from_events.description: Refresh namespace membership from namespace event objects, treating ordinary events as additions, deletion timestamps or deletion event types as removals, and ignoring names outside configured patterns.

feature2_namespace_watch_event_processin.description: Apply a single namespace watch event, ignoring initial-list events without notification and notifying when follow-up events add or remove a matching namespace.

feature3_1_resource_refresh_by_selectors.description: Refresh served resources for configured workload reactions, ignoring admission-only reactions, adding matching watchable resources, and replacing either the whole catalog or one API group depending on the refresh scope.

feature3_2_resource_refresh_ambiguous_an.description: Resolve broad resource selectors against discovered resources, excluding ambiguous specific matches, preferring the core API when a plain name collides with an extension API, allowing explicit catch-all selection, and warning when no resource matches.

feature3_3_resource_capability_filtering.description: Filter selected resources by required API capabilities, rejecting resources without list/watch support and rejecting non-patchable resources when the configured reaction needs persistent state updates.

feature4_resource_definition_event_proce.description: Apply one resource-definition watch event, ignoring initial-list events, rescanning the affected API group for follow-up changes, updating served resources from API discovery, handling disappeared groups as empty, and filling core backbone resources from core API discovery.
